entry.title: Re: Total Value Not Maching on Sales Order
entry.content:
Hello!

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • The number of decimal places is controlled by the currency configuration
  • Most currencies (e.g., EUR, USD, GBP) are set to 2 decimal places
  • The system automatically rounds any additional decimals

This is a standard system behavior and cannot be changed.

Limitation in the Public Cloud environment:

  • It is not possible to increase currency precision from 2 to 3 decimal places
  • This restriction applies system-wide and cannot be customized

You can find details in OSS Note:

3632144 - Limitation of extending Decimal Places for Currency Amounts in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Link here:  https://me.sap.com/notes/3632144/E

 

Alternative approach: 

To handle this scenario, can be used a pricing unit (scaling approach) instead of changing decimal precision.

How It Works:

Instead of maintaining:

0.123 per 1 unit

You maintain the price as:

123 per 1000 units

(or similar scaling, e.g., 12.3 per 100 units)

 

  • The system still uses 2 decimal places, but the accuracy is preserved
  • It eliminates rounding errors
  • The total amount matches the customer PO

entry.title: Re: Building a Custom Login Page for SAP Commerce Composable Storefront( JDK21 , OAUTH2)
entry.content:
Thanks for the article, this is very helpful.

I have a question regarding the security implications of hosting a custom login page in the frontend when using the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow.

In this setup, user credentials are still entered and processed within the client application. From my understanding, this seems to reduce one of the key advantages of the authorization code flow, namely that credentials are not exposed to the client.

Could you clarify how this approach addresses risks such as Cross-Site Scripting, where credentials could potentially be intercepted in the browser?

Is this approach considered equivalent in terms of security to a hosted login page on the authorization server, or are there trade-offs that should be taken into account?

entry.title: SAP Ariba Sourcing: Missing suppliers during event invitation & standalone Sourcing queries
entry.content:
Atualmente, estou enfrentando um problema relacionado à visibilidade de fornecedores no SAP Ariba Sourcing e agradeceria sua ajuda.

1. Fornecedores ausentes da lista de convidados para o evento. Ao tentar convidar fornecedores para um evento de licitação (Prospecção), o sistema não exibe todos os fornecedores do nosso banco de dados. Temos um cadastro com mais de 2.000 fornecedores. Atualmente, em nosso ambiente de QAS, estou testando com dois fornecedores específicos. Ambos possuem um relacionamento comercial ativo na rede Ariba. No entanto, apenas um deles aparece nos resultados da busca quando tento enviar o convite. Qual configuração, filtro ou etapa de sincronização pode estar faltando para garantir que todos os fornecedores cadastrados estejam visíveis e disponíveis para convite?

2. Processo de licitação de fornecedores com o módulo Standalone Sourcing. Além disso, tenho uma dúvida conceitual: quando um cliente tem apenas o módulo SAP Ariba Sourcing implementado, como exatamente o fornecedor responde a uma licitação? Ele ainda usa uma conta padrão da Ariba Network ou o fluxo de trabalho é diferente?

Agradeço antecipadamente pela sua ajuda!

entry.title: WOCR Configuration to Create Separate Warehouse Orders per Customer in Same Activity Area
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have a scenario in SAP EWM where I am picking the same product for two different customers. The key details are:

  • Two separate Outbound Delivery Orders (ODOs) are created for two different customers
  • Both ODOs are assigned to the same wave
  • Picking is happening in the same activity area
  • Automatic Pick Handling Unit (HU) creation is enabled

Requirement:
I need the system to create separate warehouse orders (WOs) for each customer, even though the product and activity area are the same.

Currently, since both deliveries are part of the same wave and activity area, they tend to get grouped together under the same WO via WOCR.

Expectation:
The WOCR should trigger in such a way that:

  • Each customer’s delivery results in a separate warehouse order
  • No mixing of warehouse tasks across customers

Question:
What configuration changes are required in WOCR (Warehouse Order Creation Rules) or related settings to achieve this behavior?
Should I use specific filters like:

  • Activity area + delivery/customer-specific parameters
  • Packing profile or HU-based grouping control
  • WOCR item filters (e.g., by document or partner)

Any guidance or best practice for handling this scenario would be helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.content:

entry.title: BTP in Rio
entry.content:
🌴BTP in Rio | Tecnologia, Estratégia e Futuro em um só lugar

No dia 30 de maio, o Rio de Janeiro recebe um encontro essencial para quem quer ir além da operação e realmente entender o papel da tecnologia na transformação dos negócios.

O BTP in Rio chega com um convite direto: sair do básico e enxergar o SAP como plataforma de inovação.

🗓Data: 30/05/2026 
⏰Horário: 09h às 14h*
📍Local: A definir 
🎤Palestrante: Marcelo Fiorito

 

O que esperar:

  • Visão prática sobre SAP BTP no cenário atual
  • Como sair do “configurador” e atuar de forma estratégica
  • Clean Core, extensões e o futuro do ecossistema SAP
  • Conexões com profissionais que estão construindo o próximo nível da carreira SAP

 

Esse não é só mais um evento técnico.
É um movimento para quem quer se posicionar com relevância no mercado SAP.

Se você trabalha com SAP e ainda não está olhando para BTP…
👉você já está atrasado.

entry.title: Re: Different Table Partitionings in S4 HANA
entry.content: Multilevel Partitioning is generally recommended when a clear performance bottleneck is observed. This can occur when tables exceed 2 billion rows, or even at lower row counts with larger data size that impacts performance.

entry.title: New Dates for Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator Live Sessions
entry.content:
Since TechEd last year, the certification for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Administrator is available. It is a system-based assessment, where you complete tasks in an authentic SAP Software environment – a real SAP BTP global account.

Currently, I am delivering the second time the Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator live session track, which introduces you to this new format of hands-on assessments and summarizes the topics covered in the certification.

Because we are seeing a high demand for these live sessions, we just added additional dates for you in July, September, and November in 2026. Please join me in the journey to become an SAP BTP Administrator.

💡Hint:
The core of the Administrating SAP Business Technology Platform learning journey leading to the certification is the Operating SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP200) course. Check-out my blog post summarizing BTP200.

entry.title: New Course Available: Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the availability of a new course on SAP Learning:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management.

👉Access the course here:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

This standalone course is designed to help professionals build strong, practical skills in configuring and managing SAP Customer Data Cloud for modern, compliant, and seamless customer identity experiences.

Why This Course?

Customer identity is at the core of trusted digital experiences. Organisations today must securely manage customer data, comply with evolving regulations, and support seamless interactions across channels and devices—all while enabling personalisation at scale.

Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management provides a comprehensive introduction to SAP Customer Data Cloud, focusing on real‑world configuration and administration tasks that directly support customer identity and access management (CIAM) scenarios.

Learning Outcome

By the end of this course, learners will be able to configure and administer SAP Customer Data Cloud to support secure and compliant customer identity management. Learners will design and manage account and data store schemas, including group models; configure site settings using the SAP Customer Data Console; and implement lite and full registration flows, authentication options, and identity journeys using UI Builder, while applying security and governance best practices.

Course Structure

The course is structured into logical units that guide learners from platform context through hands‑on configuration:

  1. SAP Customer Data Cloud in the Modern CX and Regulatory Landscape
    Understand how SAP Customer Data Cloud supports regulatory compliance and how it fits within the SAP Customer Experience portfolio.

  2. Console Site Configuration and Administration
    Learn how to navigate the SAP Customer Data Console, configure essential site settings, and work with customer data.

  3. Designing and Managing Data Schemas
    Design and manage account and data store schemas, edit schema fields, configure advanced data stores, and create group models for organizing customer accounts.

  4. Managing Customer Identity and Access
    Implement lite and full registration flows, manage identity journeys using UI Builder, and configure authentication and security options.

This structure ensures a clear progression from foundational concepts to practical identity and access management scenarios.

Who Is This Course For?

This intermediate‑level course is designed for professionals who work with SAP Customer Data Cloud and customer identity solutions, including:

  • SAP Customer Data Cloud administrators
  • Implementation consultants
  • CX professionals involved in customer identity, registration, and data management

Developer‑specific topics are addressed separately in other courses within the broader CIAM curriculum.

Part of a Growing CIAM Curriculum

This course is the first of four CIAM‑focused courses that will be added to the Learning Journey Implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud on learning.sap.com. Together, these courses will progressively expand the available self‑paced learning content and provide comprehensive coverage of SAP Customer Data Cloud capabilities.

Get Started

Whether you’re implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud for the first time or strengthening your existing expertise, Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management is the ideal place to start.

👉Enroll now:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

Stay tuned—more CIAM courses are coming soon.

entry.title: Handling Brazilian Annual Leave Reset after Sick INSS Benefit (>180 dayHi SF Experts, I have a scen
entry.content:
Hi SF Experts,

I have a scenario for a client in Brazil where the Annual Leave time account validity period must change when an employee returns from specific absences. I will use the Sick INSS Benefit as an example.

Current Setup: Annual Leave accounts have a validity of one year starting from the hire anniversary (e.g., 1 April – 31 March).

The Requirement: When an employee takes a Sick INSS Benefit absence for 180 days or more, the following must occur:

  1. Closure: The current Annual Leave account must be zeroed out (balance forfeited) and closed.

  2. Reset: Upon the employee's return, a new Annual Leave account must be created with a new accrual.

  3. Shifted Validity: The return date becomes the new anniversary date for all future periods.

Example:

  • Absence: 01 June 2026 – 30 November 2026 (183 days).

  • Action on 30 Nov: Current account is zeroed and closed.

  • Action on 01 Dec: A new account is created with validity from 01 December 2026 – 30 November 2027. All subsequent years will now follow this 01 Dec – 30 Nov cycle.

In SAP On-Premise, this is handled by program PC00_M37_QGEN.

Has anyone encountered this in SuccessFactors Time Management? If so, how did you handle the shift in validity dates? Is this achievable via standard configuration (e.g., Interim Accounts), or is it a known system limitation regarding fixed anniversary dates?

Looking forward to your insights.

Thanks,

Manon

SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central #

entry.title: Standardized OIDC API Access to SAP S/4HANA via SAP API Management and Microsoft Entra ID
entry.content:
May, 2026  |  SAP Integration Suite & Identity Security

Introduction

A recurring challenge in heterogeneous enterprise landscapes is enabling API consumers operating under a Microsoft Azure identity — authenticated via Microsoft Entra ID — to securely call SAP S/4HANA APIs without duplicating credentials, bypassing SAP's trust model, or building custom token-translation logic into every integration.

This post describes a robust, standards-based architecture that solves this challenge. SAP Integration Suite – API Management acts as the central enforcement and mediation point. SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) performs an OAuth 2.0 JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523), converting the inbound Entra ID token into an IAS-issued access token that SAP S/4HANA trusts. The result: API consumers authenticate once with Entra ID and receive governed, auditable access to S/4HANA OData and REST APIs.

Scope: This pattern addresses machine-to-machine (M2M) API access using the OAuth 2.0 client_credentials grant type — suitable for service integrations, automated pipelines, and Azure-hosted middleware that operate under a service principal registered in Microsoft Entra ID. A key S/4HANA-specific consideration is the ABAP user mapping step, which resolves incoming JWT claims to a named ABAP user on the S/4HANA system.

The Challenge: Bridging Azure Identity and SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA enforces a well-defined identity trust chain. For OIDC-based API access, the system expects tokens issued by a trusted identity provider — and will validate signature, claims, and timeliness before granting access. Beyond token validation, S/4HANA adds a critical additional step: it must resolve the token's claims to a concrete ABAP user before authorizing any API operation.

This two-layer requirement — token trust plus user mapping — makes direct presentation of a Microsoft Entra ID token to S/4HANA impractical. Entra tokens are not issued by a provider in S/4HANA's trust configuration, and the claim structure may not align with the ABAP user mapping mechanism without an explicit mediation layer.

For organizations running on-premise or private cloud S/4HANA, there is also a connectivity dimension: the API gateway on SAP BTP must reach S/4HANA through SAP Cloud Connector, adding another component to configure and operate correctly.

The pattern described here addresses all three concerns in a single, coherent design:

  • Token trust — the IAS exchange bridges Entra ID into SAP's identity model.
  • User mapping — the IAS-issued token carries the claims S/4HANA needs to resolve an ABAP user.
  • Connectivity — SAP Cloud Connector provides the secure on-premise channel from API Management to S/4HANA.

Solution Overview

The architecture connects five components across three platforms — Azure, SAP BTP, and the S/4HANA landscape. Each plays a distinct and non-overlapping role.

Component Platform Role in the Pattern

Microsoft Entra IDAzureIssues the initial JWT access token to the API consumer via the client_credentials grant. Configured as a trusted Corporate Identity Provider in IAS.
API ConsumerAzure / anyService principal or application that holds an Entra ID token and calls the SAP API Management endpoint.
SAP API ManagementSAP BTPReceives the Entra token, executes the RFC 7523 token exchange against IAS via a ServiceCallout policy, replaces the inbound token, and routes the call to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector.
SAP Identity Authentication (IAS)SAP BTP / SAP Cloud Identity ServicesValidates the Entra JWT per RFC 7523 and issues an IAS access token scoped for S/4HANA APIs.
SAP Cloud ConnectorOn-premise / Private CloudProvides the secure connectivity channel between SAP BTP (API Management) and the on-premise or private cloud S/4HANA system.
SAP S/4HANAOn-premise / Private CloudTarget API. Validates the IAS token (signature, timeliness, claims), performs ABAP user mapping, and serves the OData or REST response.

Architecture Diagram

Figure 1 — End-to-end OIDC authentication flow: Application Client (Azure) → Microsoft Entra ID → SAP API Management (BTP) → SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) → SAP Cloud Connector → SAP S/4HANA

Several trust relationships underpin this architecture:

  • Azure ↔ IAS: Microsoft Entra ID is registered as a Corporate Identity Provider in the IAS application that represents the SAP API Management integration. Entra ID is set as the default trusted issuer under Conditional Authentication for that application.
  • IAS ↔ S/4HANA: The IAS S/4HANA application holds the OAuth scopes for S/4HANA APIs and is trusted by the S/4HANA system via OIDC configuration. The dependency between the IAS APIM application and the IAS S/4HANA application (APIM2S4HANA) enables the scoped token to be issued.
  • BTP ↔ S/4HANA: SAP Cloud Connector establishes the secure tunnel between the SAP BTP subaccount hosting API Management and the S/4HANA system network.

Step-by-Step Authentication Flow

  1. Client acquires a Microsoft Entra ID access token. The API consumer authenticates to Microsoft Entra ID using the client_credentials grant type. Entra ID issues a signed JWT access token bound to the registered application's identity.
    # Step 1 — Token request to Microsoft Entra ID
POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/oauth2/v2.0/token

client_id     = {{ENTRAClientid}}
client_secret = {{ENTRASecret}}
grant_type    = client_credentials
scope         = {{scope}}
  2. Client calls the SAP API Management endpoint with the Entra token. The Entra JWT is passed in the standard Authorization: Bearer header. The API Management proxy receives the request and begins its policy chain evaluation. The client application has no knowledge of — and requires no changes for — the token exchange that follows.
    # Step 2 — Inbound call to SAP API Management
GET https://{apim-host}/api/{resource-path}
Authorization: Bearer {{EntraAccessToken}}
  3. SAP API Management performs JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523) via IAS. A ServiceCallout policy in the API proxy calls the IAS token endpoint, presenting the Entra token as the assertion and targeting the S/4HANA dependency via the resource parameter.
    # Step 3 — Token exchange: APIM calls IAS (RFC 7523)
POST https://{ias-tenant}.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

client_id     = {{IASClientid}}
client_secret = {{IASSecret}}
grant_type    = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer
assertion     = {{EntraAccessToken}}
resource      = urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:{{dependency}}
  4. SAP IAS validates the Entra JWT and issues an S/4HANA-scoped IAS token. IAS validates the inbound Entra JWT against RFC 7523 criteria — signature integrity, issuer trust, audience binding, and token expiry. Because Entra ID is configured as a trusted Corporate Identity Provider in the IAS APIM application, and the dependency link to the IAS S/4HANA application (APIM2S4HANA) is in place, IAS issues a new access token scoped for S/4HANA APIs. If validation fails at any point, IAS returns an OAuth 2.0 error response per RFC 6749.
  5. SAP API Management routes the request to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector, carrying the IAS token. The API proxy replaces the inbound Entra token with the IAS access token and forwards the request to the S/4HANA OData or REST API endpoint through SAP Cloud Connector.
    # Step 5 — Outbound call from APIM to S/4HANA (via Cloud Connector)
GET https://{virtual-host}/{odata-service-path}
Authorization: Bearer {{IASAccessToken}}
  6. SAP S/4HANA validates the IAS token and resolves the ABAP user via claim mapping. S/4HANA performs a multi-step validation and authorization sequence unique to the ABAP platform. This step is described in detail in the next section.

S/4HANA Token Validation and ABAP User Mapping

The S/4HANA side of this pattern introduces a layer of processing that is specific to the ABAP platform and has no direct equivalent in other SAP API scenarios. Understanding it is essential for a successful implementation.

What makes S/4HANA different

S/4HANA does not grant API access based solely on a valid token. After passing token validation, the system must identify a single, concrete ABAP user whose authorizations govern what the API call is permitted to do. This is the user mapping step.

When SAP S/4HANA receives the IAS access token, it executes the following sequence:

1. Token Validation

S/4HANA verifies the token against multiple criteria:

  • Digital signature check — the token signature is validated against the public keys of the trusted OIDC provider (IAS).
  • Timeliness check — the iat, exp, and nbf claims are evaluated to confirm the token is currently valid.
  • Token claim checks — audience (aud), issuer (iss), and other configured claims are verified against the OIDC trust configuration in S/4HANA.

2. ABAP User Mapping

Upon successful token validation, S/4HANA triggers user mapping. The purpose of user mapping is to resolve the token's identity to a known ABAP user account, so that ABAP authorization checks can proceed as usual.

The process works as follows:

  1. S/4HANA reads the value of the configured User Mapping Claim from the token. Common choices include the sub (subject) claim or the oid (object identifier) claim, depending on the configuration.
  2. That claim value is compared against the property selected in the User Mapping Mechanism — for example, matching against the ABAP user's logon name, email address, or a custom attribute.
  3. If exactly one ABAP user matches the claim value, that user's session is established and their ABAP authorization profile governs the API request.
  4. If no match is found, or if more than one user matches, S/4HANA rejects the request. Proper ABAP user provisioning and claim alignment are therefore prerequisites for successful authorization.

Implementation note: The IAS token claims must be aligned with the ABAP user mapping configuration in S/4HANA before deploying this pattern to production. Ensure the chosen mapping claim (e.g., sub or oid) uniquely identifies ABAP users in your landscape. Misalignment here will result in silent authorization failures that can be difficult to diagnose without OIDC-level tracing. Refer to SAP Note 3111813 for OIDC troubleshooting guidance on S/4HANA.

Key Configuration Prerequisites

1. SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) — Corporate IdP and Dependency Setup

Two IAS application registrations are required:

  • IAS SAP API Management application — The application the ServiceCallout authenticates against. Configure:
    • Grant Type: JWT Bearer
    • Trusted token issuer: Entra ID (set as the Default Corporate Identity Provider under Conditional Authentication)
    • Dependency: APIM2S4HANA — links to the IAS S/4HANA application
  • IAS S/4HANA application — Holds the OAuth scopes for S/4HANA APIs. This is the application S/4HANA trusts as its OIDC provider.

2. Microsoft Entra ID — App Registration

Register an application in Microsoft Entra ID for the API consumer service principal. Configure the client_credentials grant and assign the appropriate scope. Entra ID must be set as the Corporate Identity Provider in the IAS application configuration so that the RFC 7523 exchange can proceed.

3. SAP Cloud Connector — Virtual Host Mapping

For on-premise or private cloud S/4HANA systems, SAP Cloud Connector must be configured to expose the S/4HANA host as a virtual host accessible from the SAP BTP subaccount where API Management is deployed. The API Management target endpoint in the API proxy uses this virtual host address.

4. SAP S/4HANA — OIDC Trust Configuration and User Mapping

Required: SAP S/4HANA must be configured to trust IAS as an OIDC provider and the ABAP user mapping mechanism must be aligned with the claims in the IAS-issued token. Refer to SAP Note 3111813 (OpenID Connect: Troubleshooting Note) for configuration guidance and diagnostic procedures.

5. SAP API Management — Policy Chain

The API proxy requires two policy steps:

  1. A ServiceCallout policy to call the IAS token endpoint with the RFC 7523 parameters (as shown in Step 3 above).
  2. A Set Authorization: Bearer / Route to S4HANA API policy to replace the inbound Entra token with the IAS token and forward the call via the Cloud Connector virtual host.

Why This Pattern Works

The design is effective because it preserves the integrity of each platform's identity model while creating a clean, opaque mediation layer at the API gateway:

  • No SAP credentials in the Azure estate. The API consumer never holds S/4HANA or IAS credentials. It authenticates exclusively with Entra ID using its existing service principal — reducing credential sprawl and audit surface.
  • Standards-based, auditable exchange. RFC 7523 is a published IETF standard. The token exchange is deterministic, well-documented, and can be traced at each hop using standard OAuth 2.0 logging.
  • Single policy enforcement point. Token exchange and routing logic reside entirely in the API Management policy chain — not duplicated across client applications. Changing the token exchange configuration requires updating one policy, not every consumer.
  • Transparent to API consumers. The client presents an Entra token and receives S/4HANA data. All mediation — token exchange, routing via Cloud Connector, ABAP user resolution — is invisible to the consumer.
  • S/4HANA's ABAP authorization model is preserved. Because user mapping resolves to a real ABAP user, all existing ABAP role and authorization object assignments apply to every API call. No bypass of the ABAP authorization layer occurs.

Standards and References

Reference Description

RFC 7523JSON Web Token (JWT) Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants — defines the urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer grant type used for the IAS token exchange.
RFC 6749The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework — baseline specification governing token request and error response formats.
OpenID Connect (OIDC)Identity layer on OAuth 2.0. Both Microsoft Entra ID and SAP IAS issue OIDC-compliant JWTs; S/4HANA's OIDC trust configuration references IAS as its provider.
SAP Note 3111813OpenID Connect (OIDC): Troubleshooting Note for SAP S/4HANA. Covers OIDC trust configuration, token validation diagnostics, and ABAP user mapping setup. Essential reading before deployment.

Part of a Broader Pattern Series

This scenario is part of a series documenting standardized OIDC-based API access patterns across the SAP portfolio using SAP API Management as the common enforcement layer. A companion blog post (Link) covers the same RFC 7523 JWT exchange pattern applied to SAP SuccessFactors APIs, where the S/4HANA-specific Cloud Connector connectivity and ABAP user mapping steps are replaced by the SuccessFactors IAS trust configuration (SAP Note 3681224). The core token exchange mechanics — and the IAS dependency model — are identical across both scenarios.

Conclusion

Exposing SAP S/4HANA APIs to Azure-native consumers does not require abandoning SAP's identity trust model or introducing platform-specific credential stores on the Azure side. The combination of SAP API Management, SAP Identity Authentication (IAS), and RFC 7523 JWT Bearer Token Exchange provides a clean, standards-based bridge that preserves the security posture of both platforms.

For on-premise and private cloud S/4HANA deployments, SAP Cloud Connector completes the connectivity picture without requiring S/4HANA to be directly internet-reachable. And the ABAP user mapping step ensures that S/4HANA's granular role-based authorization model continues to govern every API call — regardless of where the original identity token originated.

Teams building Azure-integrated SAP landscapes should consider this pattern as a reusable building block, applicable wherever an Entra ID service principal needs governed access to S/4HANA data through a centrally managed API gateway.

Related resources:
SAP Note 3111813 — OpenID Connect (OIDC): Troubleshooting Note for SAP S/4HANA
SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication documentation
SAP API Management documentation
SAP Cloud Connector documentation
IETF RFC 7523 — JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0
IETF RFC 6749 — The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework

entry.title: Re: MM Period Issue
entry.content:
Dear @AjayMSL,

In Public Cloud the March FI posting period does not have to close automatically in May if the business still needs to post March adjustments. The supported approach is to maintain the open posting period range in Manage Posting Periods F2293 and adjust the recurrence/job logic so that March remains open until the required cut-off date

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3371464

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F2293')/S36

This should be handled in the application, not via CBC or by using the technical GL job template

Important limitation

If the requirement is about MM/material document posting periods the logic is different. SAP Public Cloud learning content states that usually only the current MM posting period is open, backposting is only possible to the previous period if allowed and once a period is closed it is no longer possible to post to what has become the period before last

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/opening-new-posting-period_e58522bf-3ba2-4b88-a896-efbe59973965

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Customer has overdue items pending but still delivery is being allowed
entry.content:
• Credit check simulation shows “Percentage 0% of overdue open items under 0% ( days)” (Message no. UKM_CHECK035) even though overdue open items exist.
  • Credit check is passed for the items although there are overdue open items for the customer.
  • Credit check step 130 Check for Overdue Open Items is not able to block the customer items although the open items are overdue for the customer.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Customer has overdue items pending but still delivery...
entry.content:
The overdue grid is determined based on the configuration maintained in “Define Credit Segment.” If the calculated amount for any due-date bucket is negative, it is excluded from consideration. Therefore, even if there are overdue items, they will be ignored if their total results in a negative value.

Refer to: Aging Grid in Credit Management

entry.title: Offer and Start
entry.content:
Welcome to the fourth blog in our series on using SAP Fieldglass features to detect candidate fraud. In this series, we're exploring how to identify fraudulent applicants at every stage of the hiring process. If you haven't already, we recommend reviewing our previous blogs: Candidate Selection, Preparing for the Interview, and During the Hiring Process. 

Imagine you’ve navigated through a sea of resumes, conducted countless interviews, and finally extended an offer to your dream candidate. But what if, beneath the perfect responses, the reality turns out to be different? The offer and start phases are critical stages where candidate fraud can still happen, even in a secure system like SAP Fieldglass.  

The offer and start phases are your final opportunity to catch issues before you invest in a new worker. Even in a secure system like SAP Fieldglass, candidate fraud can still occur at this stage. The following are signs that could indicate a problem: 

  • Pushback on background checks 
  • Documents that appear altered 
  • Urgency to start right away 
  • Mismatched signatures 
  • Inconsistent banking details  
  • Refusal to appear on camera for onboarding 
  • Avoidance to provide employment verification contacts 

Let's check some SAP Fieldglass features that can help you detect fraudulent activities and support a secure onboarding process. 

Custom Fields for Extra Security Checks 

If you need to track information beyond the standard SAP Fieldglass fields, you can create custom fields. Custom fields with security checks help prevent candidates from using fake, stolen, or borrowed identities.   

For example, you can create a custom field that requires the supplier to input the candidate's government ID number. This field can be linked to a validation rule that cross-checks against official databases or previous records in the system. This approach helps detect duplicate IDs or situations where one person applies under multiple names.   

You can also add an employment history verification field requiring suppliers to upload certified reference letters. Include sub-fields for employer name, employment dates, job title, and direct contact information. When a mismatch occurs between what the candidate claimed and what previous employers confirm, it can indicate false experience or fabricated positions.

Another option is to create fields for role-specific certifications, such as a current first-aid certificate or a professional license number. Include a field requiring suppliers to confirm they've physically verified original documents. You can also add a checkbox to confirm that the certificate is valid as of a given date and matches official registry records. 

All custom field entries create a documented audit trail. This holds suppliers accountable and provides evidence if fraud is discovered later. 

To add a new custom field: 

  1. Go to Admin Menu 
  2. Select Configuration 
  3. Select Custom Field 
  4. Select New 
  5. Choose a module and fill in the required information. 

eSignature with DocuSign 

During the offer and start phase, it's important to verify document integrity and validate identities through authentication methods such as ID verification or secure, personalized email. 

The DocuSign feature in SAP Fieldglass can house multiple types of documents that require signature management between buyers, suppliers, and workers. By using eSignature with DocuSign, you can confirm that the person signing the contract or onboarding documents is indeed the candidate. This reduces the risk of identity fraud or document changes during the start phase. 

DocuSign provides a full audit trail for all signed documents, protecting against unauthorized modifications. Note that your company needs at least one DocuSign account to configure with the SAP Fieldglass system. 

Now that we've covered features that can help you during the offer and start phases, let's move to the last blog in the series and see how you can protect your organization during the post-hiring phase. 

 

 

For the purposes of this guide, "candidate fraud" means deliberate misrepresentation of identity, credentials, work history, or qualifications during recruitment. This guide is preventive and educational; it does not make legal determinations.

entry.title: New Practice System for Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100)
entry.content:
Last year, we took the practice system for the Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100) course offline to upgrade its capabilities to better reflect the latest additions to the course material. This was right before our latest innovation was kicked off internally: SAP Certifications reimagined.

Unfortunately, this shift of priorities led to some delay for this practice system. But now it is here: SAP Fiori – Foundation

  • You get full administrative access to an SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS04 like you would get in the classroom version of the UX100.
  • You get full administrative access to an SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP Business Application Studio and SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

All exercises can be done without any limitation:

  • Explore a fresh installed SAP S/4HANA with SAP Best Practices in place.
  • Use the fully configured SAP Fiori launchpad.
  • Set up SAP Fiori spaces, pages, and catalogs.
  • Dive into SAP Fiori development and system administration.

It is all here, just one booking away. Are you ready for some exercise(s)?

💡Hint:
UX100 is part of the learning journeys Developing SAPUI5 Applications and SAP Fiori – System Administration, which both lead to a certification. Check-out my blog post summarizing UX100.

entry.title: From Idea to Possibility: Internal Tool Development with SAP BTP, CAP, and Claude Code
entry.content:
Internal teams often understand their own challenges very clearly. Enterprise Architects, IT consultants, business teams, and technical teams all see opportunities where a small internal tool could improve processes, structure information, or support better decisions.

However, turning these ideas into working applications is not always easy. It usually requires knowledge of backend development, frontend structure, databases, deployment, security, and SAP-specific technologies.

This is where SAP BTP, CAP, Cloud Foundry, and SAP Business Application Studio open interesting possibilities.

With these technologies, teams with access to SAP BTP can explore SAP-native internal tools with a structured setup: frontend, backend, services, database persistence, and cloud deployment. Instead of staying only at the idea or slide level, teams can start imagining how their concepts could become real prototypes inside SAP BTP.

Possible internal use cases could include:

  • capability mapping tools
  • architecture decision tracking
  • lightweight reporting dashboards
  • internal knowledge repositories
  • recommendation-based guidance tools
  • structured stakeholder input tools
  • portfolio or transformation tracking tools
  • process support and data collection tools

After deployment, an application can run inside SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and be accessed through the SAP BTP Cockpit:

SAP BTP Cockpit → Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Applications → Selected App → Mapped Route

From there, teams can monitor application status, memory usage, service bindings, instances, and routes. This shows how an idea can move beyond a local prototype and become a cloud-deployed enterprise application.

A useful addition to this setup is Claude Code. It can act as an AI-assisted development helper by supporting CAP project structure, CDS models, service definitions, Node.js handlers, mta.yaml configuration, deployment setup, and debugging.

The main point is not that AI replaces developers. Instead, it can help Enterprise Architects, IT consultants, semi-technical users, and cross-functional teams better understand what is possible, prototype faster, and collaborate more effectively with development teams.

SAP BTP, CAP, Cloud Foundry, BAS, and AI-assisted development show a future where internal tool ideas can move faster from concept to possibility  while still staying aligned with SAP-native technologies

#SAP #SAPBTP #CAP #CloudFoundry #SAPBusinessApplicationStudio #ClaudeCode #AIAssistedDevelopment #EnterpriseArchitecture #ITConsulting #EnterpriseTools #InternalTools #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: Powering unique engagement at Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Thousands of marketers and CX leaders are heading to Sapphire in Orlando and Madrid this May to join SAP leaders and industry peers for the reveal of a reimagined Joule experience — and our bold new vision for how businesses will run from now on. SAP Engagement Cloud will share exciting innovations and give attendees a front-row seat to how agentic AI is reshaping customer engagement across every channel.

Closing the engagement divide

At Sapphire, you'll hear speakers reference the Engagement Divide — the growing gap between what customers expect and what brands can actually deliver. Research from the 2026 Global Engagement Index Report shows this divide is widening at an unprecedented pace, and AI is compounding it faster than most organizations realize.

The numbers are stark: only 22% of brands recognise they have a problem creating seamless experiences, yet 82% of consumers are dissatisfied with brands. The leading cause? 78% of brands say they cannot manage campaigns with real-time AI optimisation to personalise at scale. This isn't just a marketing problem — it's a fundamental, business-wide challenge.

This year, SAP Engagement Cloud will showcase how B2B and B2C organisations can close this divide by building a foundation that keeps pace with AI and rising consumer demand.

Show floor: a unified customer engine

Head to the demo pods to see the engagement transformation live. The SAP Customer Experience portfolio uses agentic AI to unify customer and operational data, personalise engagement across channels, adapt loyalty programmes in real time, and turn interactions into advocacy.

Orlando - Pod CXP2457 — Marketing and Engagement

Madrid - Pod CXP1461 — Marketing and Engagement

Must-attend sessions

Sapphire Orlando

From campaigns to AI-driven marketing with SAP and Google Cloud

SAP2674 · Tue May 12, 1:30pm · Kelsey Jones, Head of Product Marketing, SAP

Personalisation: data-driven, AI-enhanced engagement that builds loyalty

CXP1499 · Tue May 12, 2:30pm · Kelsey Jones, SAP; Jonathan Martz, CRM Manager, Gibson

[Keynote] Dive in or drift: surfing the agentic customer experience revolution

KEY2518 · Wed May 13, 1:00pm · Balaji Balasubramanian & Jessica Keehn, SAP Customer Experience

Sapphire Madrid

How adidas drives AdiClub member loyalty at scale with SAP Engagement Cloud

CXP1219 · Wed May 20, 2:00pm · Sasan Naservafai & Lorenzo Maggi, SAP

[Keynote] Dive in or drift: surfing the agentic customer experience revolution

KEY1428 · Thu May 21, 11:30am · Balaji Balasubramanian & Jessica Keehn, SAP Customer Experience

Frequently asked questions

I'm not a marketer — are these sessions still relevant for me?

Yes! The suggested sessions are perfect for someone in IT or commerce-related roles as well.

In what sessions will I get to see a demo?

You'll see SAP Engagement Cloud demos at the show floor demo pods: CXP2457 in Orlando and CXP1461 in Madrid, both in the Marketing and Engagement zone. Demos cover unifying customer and operational data with agentic AI to personalise engagement, adapt loyalty programmes in real time, and drive scalable growth.

What product innovations will be showcased at Sapphire?

There will be a reveal of a reimagined Joule experience and our bold new vision for how businesses will run from now on.

I can't attend in person — is any content available virtually?

Yes! Join us May 12–13 at SAP Sapphire Virtual for keynotes, strategy talks, ask the experts, solution deep dives, and road map reviews.

entry.title: Re: Validation Error: Exception Subprocess should start w...
entry.content: Exception subprocess cannot have two error ends. It must have only one end type—either an error end or an end message. Consider moving the router logic inside the LIP

entry.title: Re: New Dates for Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator Live Sessions
entry.content:
@mts2605 The automatic verification checks your work step by step starting with the directories and subaccounts. If the name or the subdomain is not what is written in the task document, the verification stops with 0%. The reason is quite simple: To check what is actually inside of a subaccount, the verification needs a correct subdomain to access it.

💡Hints:

  • Be accurate in everything you enter.
  • Use copy & paste as often as possible.
  • When copying, check for technical characters like line-breaks.

We are currently tidying up the practice systems. It should hopefully be gone for your S-user in the near future.
